title: Peregrino

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Peregrino as an isometric survival management game centered on crossing a cursed land, scavenging, hunting, fighting, managing scarce resources, inventory pressure, nightfall danger, and extracting with loot. The horror identity is strong via grotesque abominations, cursed woods, and survival-focused threat escalation after dusk. The game also has clear party/caravan management elements: caring for companions, leading pilgrims, making fate-affecting decisions, and upgrading caravan crafting stations. That makes it broader than pure survival horror, so survival_horror fits best as the primary archetype with tactical_rpg and party_based_crpg as secondary influences rather than full identities.

overall_note: The rail has a solid center of gravity around party or caravan survival under attrition, with several candidates matching Peregrino's travel-management loop well. Best fits emphasize expedition structure, supplies, companion pressure, and difficult decisions; weaker fits drift toward formal turn-based tactics or roguelike combat loops over survival management.
anchor_summary: Peregrino is an isometric single-player survival management journey built around leading a caravan/party through a hostile horror landscape, balancing scavenging, resource scarcity, camp or caravan upkeep, companion care, route risk, and tactical survival decisions rather than pure action-horror.
gap_notes: The strongest overlaps are caravan or group survival-management games; the set is less consistent on Peregrino's day exploration/night refuge cadence.
Several candidates match tone and journey structure better than they match the anchor's scavenging, extraction, and inventory-pressure loop.
The rail leans somewhat too heavily on turn-based tactical RPGs, which captures party pressure but can miss the anchor's survival-management identity.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close on caravan leadership, harsh travel, supply management, crew welfare, route planning, and survival in a hostile world, though it is more narrative CRPG/trading oriented than horror-survival.

Excellent match on scarcity, shelter upkeep, survivor needs, day/night pressure, scavenging runs, and hard moral survival choices, though it lacks the anchor's caravan-trek structure and broader party expedition framing.
Strong caravan-journey and follower-management overlap with consequential survival decisions, but its identity is more narrative tactical RPG than systemic scavenging and survival management.

Good fit on leading a roaming party through a hostile world with camp upkeep, resources, crafting, and attrition, but the core loop is more open-world mercenary tactics than horror survival management.

Shares bleak road-trip structure, party stress, coach upgrades, and horror-driven attrition, but its run-based roguelike and combat-forward design is a looser fit than games centered on survival logistics and caravan management.

title: Pieced Together

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present a short, single-player scrapbook puzzler built around sorting mementos, reassembling torn photos, and solving gentle logic/word puzzles across handcrafted pages while uncovering an authored story about friendship and aging. There is no combat, the tone is explicitly cozy and emotional, and interaction appears cursor-driven through scrapbook pages. While the copy does not strongly emphasize classic hidden-object searching, the closest supported taxonomy fit is hidden_object_puzzle because the core loop is light object-focused puzzle interaction in handcrafted scenes/pages with narrative progression.

overall_note: The rail has a solid grasp of the anchor's cozy, object-centered, narrative-puzzle identity. The top three are strong fits, but the back half drifts toward narrative-adventure and interactive-fiction comps that share mood and memoir themes more than the same core loop.
anchor_summary: Pieced Together is a very short, single-player cozy narrative puzzler built around sorting keepsakes, reassembling photos, solving gentle logic/word tasks, and decorating scrapbook pages to uncover an intimate authored story. Best matches should share low-pressure object manipulation, light puzzle gating, handcrafted progression, and story delivery through personal items rather than action or broad exploration.
gap_notes: Top candidates appropriately focus on tactile object interaction, sorting, arrangement, and gentle authored progression.
The set leans a bit too heavily on emotional/artistic tone in lower ranks; similarity should stay anchored in light object-puzzle play rather than memoir presentation alone.
No candidate is a base-game pairing or near-identical gameplay match, so the rail is good but not excellent.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close fit: cozy single-player object manipulation, environmental organization, gentle puzzle structure, decoration, and intimate story revealed through personal belongings.
Excellent match on casual tactile sorting/arrangement puzzles and low-pressure play, though it is less narrative-led and less memoir-focused than the anchor.
Short, cozy, story-driven puzzle game centered on handling meaningful objects; the repair/disassembly focus differs, but the emotional authored progression is very compatible.

title: Mirage 7

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present Mirage 7 as a third-person, single-player story adventure centered on Nadira’s journey through a desert setting, with authored narration, environmental puzzle solving, collectible-item crafting, and melee/ranged combat against monsters. That combination supports a cinematic action-adventure identity more strongly than RPG or open-world labels. World topology is not stated explicitly, but the copy emphasizes a guided tale and handcrafted challenges rather than sandbox or systemic play.

overall_note: The set captures Mirage 7's general third-person story-adventure identity reasonably well, especially on exploration, puzzles, and accessible action. Best fits are authored action-adventures with environmental traversal and light progression; weaker fits drift toward pure puzzle adventure, stealth-driven structure, or open-world action RPG.
anchor_summary: Mirage 7 appears to be a single-player third-person cinematic action-adventure: a guided story journey with environmental puzzle solving, light crafting/tool use, and melee/ranged combat against monsters, rather than an open-world RPG or pure puzzle game.
gap_notes: The rail leans a bit too broad between puzzle-adventure and action RPG; Mirage 7 seems more tightly in the cinematic action-adventure middle.
A stronger set would emphasize guided third-person narrative adventures with both environmental puzzles and modest melee/ranged combat, not just shared mood or setting.

title: Fortuna Magus

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored DB evidence consistently describes Fortuna Magus as a fantasy JRPG with a rich authored story, single-player campaign structure, and strategic turn-based party battles. Core gameplay details are concrete and repeated across Steam short/detailed descriptions and Metacritic: characters pursue a missing-father narrative, battles revolve around Revelations, TP management, tandem attacks, and learning new spells/skills through combat conditions and element thresholds. That supports a story-led JRPG identity with party management, magic-focused turn-based combat, quest-driven progression, and skill customization. Alternative candidates like turn_based_tactics are weaker because there is no evidence of grid tactics or overhead tactical structure, while monster_collect_rpg lacks creature-collection evidence.

overall_note: The set stays mostly on-target for story-driven turn-based JRPGs. The best fits are other compact, linear party-based JRPGs with strong progression systems, especially Kemco-style titles. A few candidates are solid but broader retro-JRPG neighbors rather than especially tight gameplay matches.
anchor_summary: Fortuna Magus is a single-player, story-led turn-based JRPG built around party management, authored progression, skill customization, and battle-specific resource/condition systems like TP, tandem attacks, elemental growth, and unlockable abilities.
gap_notes: The rail would be stronger with more direct Kemco/EXE-CREATE-style turn-based JRPG comps centered on compact campaign structure and layered skill unlock systems.
Top candidates fit the anchor's JRPG identity well, but not all reflect the anchor's specific combat-condition learning and elemental progression hooks.
No obvious bad mismatch is present, but some picks are more general retro-JRPG comparisons than especially close structural matches.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close fit: traditional fantasy turn-based JRPG, fixed party progression, linear story campaign, and Kemco-style character/skill system emphasis align strongly with Fortuna Magus.

title: Messy Up

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence establishes a cute multiplayer pet-chaos game with co-op, PvP, and asymmetric roles (pets cause destruction, owner catches/defends), plus collectibles and a home-building mode. However, the material does not clearly anchor the game to any allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. The strongest signals are party/co-op/social chaos, but the evidence does not concretely define a match-based party-game structure, nor does it support action-adventure, platformer, shooter, racer, sim, or RPG archetypes in the allowed list. The current taxonomy audit already flagged a provider gap with no candidate archetypes. Because the gameplay description is broad, mode-mixed, and not strongly aligned to a supported archetype, classification should be rejected for now.

overall_note: The candidate set captures the anchor's strongest gameplay identity: animal-led chaos, household/environment interaction, accessible multiplayer, and slapstick disruption. Best fits are games built around mischievous animals and sandbox havoc; weaker fits drift toward pure brawler-party or task-coordination structures rather than asymmetric pet-destruction play.
anchor_summary: Messy Up is a casual multiplayer pet-chaos game about cats and dogs wrecking household spaces, with up to 3-player co-op plus an asymmetric owner-vs-pets PvP angle. title: Banquet for Fools

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present Banquet for Fools as a single-player, open-world, party-based RPG centered on investigating a village disappearance across the island of Invimona. Core loop evidence is strong: exploring towns and holds for clues, taking quests/requests, managing four companions with stat-driven dialogue options and bonding, and progressing characters through skills, spells, songs, equipment, and level-ups. Combat is explicitly described as brawl-style beat-'em-up action with pausable party command decisions, leader switching, support combos, and coordinated attacks, supporting a western_narrative_rpg classification with beat_em_up as a secondary flavor rather than the primary identity. The mystery-driven quest structure, party management, authored campaign framing, and open-world exploration are all directly evidenced in the Steam detailed description and echoed by the short description/Metacritic copy.

overall_note: The rail has a solid center around party-based real-time RPGs with exploration and companion management, but several picks drift toward either much more classic CRPG structure or looser single-character action RPGs. The best fits are games combining party control, open-area exploration, and pause-capable or hybrid real-time combat.
anchor_summary: Banquet for Fools appears to be a single-player open-world party action RPG with a mystery-solving quest spine, four-character party management, exploratory island travel, and brawl-style real-time combat that includes pausing for command selection, leader switching, and combo coordination. Its identity leans more toward western party-based narrative action RPG than JRPG, with investigation and navigation challenge as notable differentiators.

title: Cupiclaw

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Cupiclaw as a 'cute & frantic claw machine roguelike deckbuilder' centered on grabbing prizes, building combos, customizing runs, upgrading the claw, and facing increasingly chaotic machines. That is enough to establish a clear gameplay identity, but the identity does not cleanly map to any allowed archetype in the taxonomy. It is not supported as sports_sim, realistic_racer, or pinball despite the current computed near-misses, and it also does not have sufficient evidence of card-battle combat to justify card_battler. Because the core loop appears to be an arcade claw-machine roguelike/deckbuilding hybrid outside the available archetype set, rejection is more appropriate than forcing a misleading fit.

overall_note: The rail has a solid grasp of Cupiclaw's unusual hybrid identity: arcade-device interaction plus roguelike combo-building. Best candidates emphasize physics/board-driven runs and emergent synergies. The main weakness is that some picks drift toward combat-centric roguelites rather than prize-machine or score-engine style play.
anchor_summary: Cupiclaw is a single-player arcade/strategy roguelike deckbuilder built around claw-machine interactions: grabbing prizes, assembling combo synergies, upgrading the claw, and surviving increasingly chaotic run-based machines. The closest fits should share run-based drafting/buildcraft and a tactile physics/arcade board layer, not just generic roguelite structure.
gap_notes: Best fits are games where the central action is operating or aiming within a machine/board system while drafting synergies across runs.
Combat-heavy roguelites with enemies and class abilities are somewhat less faithful than score/combo engine builders built around arcade randomness.
No candidate is a near-identical match for the claw-machine-specific interaction, so even the top pick is a strong neighbor rather than 'same'.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close on run-based drafting, arcade-machine feel, kinetic board interactions, and absurd combo engines. Pachinko scoring differs from claw grabbing, but the physics-plus-synergy identity is highly compatible.

Excellent match on roguelike deckbuilder structure, randomized drafting, and payoff-combo construction inside a gambling-machine frame. Less tactile/physics-driven than Cupiclaw, but very aligned in buildcraft and run logic.

Shares physics-driven aiming, run progression, relic/orb synergies, and arcade roots. However, Peglin is fundamentally turn-based enemy combat, which is a meaningful step away from Cupiclaw's machine-management combo loop.

The pinball physics and roguelite runs are relevant, but Roundguard is primarily a dungeon crawler with character classes, loot, and combat encounters. That makes it less faithful to Cupiclaw's prize-machine/deckbuilder identity.

title: Bulb Boy 2: Jar of Despair

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store/metadata evidence consistently describes Bulb Boy 2 as a single-player point-and-click horror adventure built around solving tricky puzzles, navigating eerie handcrafted spaces, and unfolding a linear twisted story. Core signals are strong for cursor-driven interaction, environmental puzzle solving, authored linear progression, mystery/survival-escape framing, and essentially non-combat-first play. The text does mention facing jelly monsters and a boss, so I treated combat as light rather than none, but the dominant gameplay identity still aligns best with hidden_object_puzzle / point-and-click puzzle adventure. Horror tone is strong but not enough to override the puzzle-first structure as the primary archetype.
